---
title: "Wake Up Babe, New Quarto Product Just Dropped"
author: "Tinashe M. Tapera"
date: "2026-04-15"
image: "FB6D95E2-9380-4DBB-856D-3D92D9BBC6A6.jpeg"
categories: ["writing", "python", "documentation", "quarto", "great-docs"]
title-block-banner: true
filters:
   - lightbox
lightbox: auto
---

Posit just announced the release of their new Quarto-based Python package documentation site generator: [`great-docs`](https://posit-dev.github.io/great-docs/).

::: {.callout-note}
[Click here to skip to the tutorial](#ready-to-try-out-great-docs)
:::

`great-docs` promises to inspect your Python package and generate a beautiful, fully featured documentation website, featuring:

- API reference
- CLI reference 
- A snazzy landing page
- Space for user guides, blog posts, tutorials, dev notes, notebooks, etc.

In the R world, we’ve been blessed for many years with [`pkgdown`](https://pkgdown.r-lib.org/), 
and I’ll be honest — it's one of my top reasons to start a multilingual project in R 
as opposed to Python. With `pkgdown`, you use [Roxygen tags](https://roxygen2.r-lib.org/reference/tags-rd.html)
(analogous to Python decorators) to annotate your functions, and as `pkgdown` scans your 
R scripts, it pulls out the tags and fills out R package-compliant documentation files. 
Once these files are complete, `pkgdown` automates the generation of a 
package documentation website, which can then be hosted by GitHub Pages or otherwise. 
Today, `pkgdown` sites are ubiquitous and instantly identifiable, and — in my opinion — are
a strong indicator of R code quality and attention to detail.

Of course, Python has documentation management for a long time, too. 
The first time I documented a Python package was with [Sphinx-doc](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/), 
and it was...alright. It was certainly more work than Roxygen, 
requiring an entirely unique phase of development to generate, tidy up, and deploy. 
Since then, numerous other documentation mechanisms have popped up thanks to 
Python’s various docstrings specifications, including [Google Style](https://google.github.io/styleguide/pyguide.html), 
[Numpy](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/), [Sphinx](https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/), [Epytext](https://epytext.readthedocs.io/en/latest/), 
and possibly more I'm unaware of. One of my gripes with Python is that 
because it is so easy to publish new things, Python is a bit of an "anything goes," "wild west" language, 
and the documentation space is no different. Each time someone has attempted to 
establish, "the next best docstring format," we’ve ended up back in the 
"conflicting standards," comic all over again:

![XKCD conflicting standards comic](https://imgs.xkcd.com/comics/standards.png)

Now, we have `great-docs`. So why will this time around be any different? Here’s my opinion:

## `great-docs` is NOT a Docstring Style Specification

Despite what I initially thought, the Posit team is not suggesting we 
adopt a new specification. In my opinion, that decision would be 
counterproductive (as XKCD rightly points out). Instead, `great-docs` is 
a Quarto-driven _documentation generator_. Instead of trying to 
convince the Python community to change its ways yet again, 
and writing a brand new parser for a a new decorator style, 
`great-docs` accepts multiple docstring styles and implements a 
universal parser to ingest them, spitting out a unified API documentation
that any Python user can read and easily understand. 
At present, the `great-docs` documentation tells us that it 
accepts (and automatically detects😱) `numpy`, Google, and Sphinx styles. 

## `great-docs` is Driven By Quarto

Quarto is certainly picking up speed in the data science community, 
with the number of adopters increasing all the time. 

![Google Search Trends for Quarto+Posit](quarto-search-trend.png)

The easier it is to integrate Quarto into people’s existing software 
development infrastructure, the more confidence I have that this will 
be a worthwhile project. Furthermore, because Quarto has rich 
and accessible [extension functionality](https://quarto.org/docs/extensions/), 
I have no doubt that we’re about to see an explosion of 
creative ideas and tools.[^1]

[^1]: The extension ecosystem is already exploding, check out the [`awesome-quarto`](https://github.com/mcanouil/awesome-quarto) GitHub repo for some interesting examples.

## Pseudo-Corporate Backing

Hear me out — as an academic and member of the FOSS community, I am 
weary of anytime a company promotes a package as a standard for an 
open source language. However, in this case, we’re talking about Posit PBC, 
which is a [registered non-profit](https://posit.co/about). In case you’re unaware, Hadley Wickham 
is the tech-brain behind the venture, with JJ Allaire and Tareef Kawaf serving
as co-founders. Wickham started out as an R developer back when the company
was called RStudio. Today, RStudio is synonymous with R itself, 
so if you’ve trusted RStudio this far as a company, I’d bet you can 
trust Posit, and by extension, their products, Quarto being one of their flagships.

## Multilinguists Will Now Have An Easy On-Ramp

There are a lot of R users who feel skittish about Python. 
Since they normally come from the statistics world, and Python sits squarely in the software 
engineers' world, R users sometimes have a hard time getting 
into the Python community. Fortunately, Posit has positioned 
itself as a polyglot data science developer company first and foremost[^2]. As a result, 
its R userbase may be more likely to get their feet wet with Python, 
since they already know Quarto and will have a sense of familiarity with 
Posit’s approach to open, welcoming, multilingual data science. 
Likewise, [Pythonistas](https://medium.com/byte-sized-code/are-you-a-pythonista-197debd5efa6) 
might get more involved in the world of R, since the two communities are starting to overlap more and more.

## Verdict

I’m _obviously_ biased, as a fan of everything Posit has done. 
However, I am still trepidatious. I haven’t fully adopted Positron as my IDE, 
mainly because I’ve not had error free integration of remote-SSH; I work on 
HPC compute clusters in academia, so that is non-negotiable. 
I acknowledge, though, that this may not exactly be Positrons shortcoming, 
but instead due to the fact that each HPC cluster is different and requires 
a niche implementation of the IDEs it can run. Additionally, I didn’t have 
tremendous luck connecting to a MySQL Server instance a few weeks ago.

That being said, everything else I’ve used from Posit has been damn near 
excellent, and has only added positives to my data journey. I’m certainly 
going to embrace `great-docs`, and I've already figured out how to
use it to replace my previous notebook-driven development tool, `nbdev`. 
With a few couple simple Quarto extensions, we can create beautiful,
expressive notebook-driven documentation with `great-docs`.

[^2]: Check out Wes McKinney's [talk at `jupytercon`](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wdmf1msbtVs) where he talks about how Posit is firmly committed to supporting the Python community, and how develop polyglot data science tools. 

## Ready to try out `great-docs`?

Getting started with `great-docs` is dead easy. In my case, 
I’m going to combine this with the extensions `sorting-hat` —
which selectively includes or excludes code blocks based on 
programming language and cell — and `ripper` — which allows you to 
extract cells to individual files — to do some **notebook-driven development**. 

<details>
Notebook-driven development is a software development workflow where you write your code in 
Jupyter or Quarto notebooks, and then extract that code into script that 
can be imported as modules. By keeping the notebook coupled to the final output module,
you reap all of the benefits of [Donald Knuth's _literate programming_ paradigm](http://literateprogramming.com/).
</details>

Let's get started by creating a Quarto project:

```bash
quarto create project default great-docs-demo great-docs-demo --no-prompt --no-open
```

Next, we need to set up our Python environment. I recommend
doing this with [`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/), which is a Python package manager that 
will help us manage our dependencies and virtual environment for this project:

```bash
cd great-docs-demo

# important: great-docs requires Python > 3.11
uv init --lib .

# use uv to create a new virtual environment and install nbdev
uv venv --python 3.13
source .venv/bin/activate
```

With `uv` ready, install and initialize `great-docs`, and to
use notebooks, install `ipykernel` and `jupyter`:

```bash
uv pip install great-docs ipykernel jupyter

great-docs init

uv add great-docs quarto jupyter ipykernel

# dont forget to git
git add .
git commit -am "Initialize project"
```

At this point, you should have something like this:

```
(great-docs-demo) (base) ➜  great-docs-demo git:(main) tree -L 3
.
├── README.md
├── _quarto.yml
├── great-docs-demo.qmd
├── great-docs.yml
├── pyproject.toml
├── src
│   └── great_docs_demo
│       ├── __init__.py
│       └── py.typed
└── uv.lock

3 directories, 8 files
```

It looks a little like a Python package, but there's still more to do. 
To make notebooks useful for documentation, add the Quarto
extensions `sorting-hat` and `ripper`, and add two 
directories `user_guide` and `dev` to hold the notebooks:

```bash
quarto add coatless-quarto/ripper
quarto add coatless-quarto/sorting-hat

mkdir user_guide dev

git add .
git commit -m "Add Quarto extensions for notebook-driven documentation"
```


::: {.callout-tip}

The `user_guide` directory is [reserved in `great-docs`](https://posit-dev.github.io/great-docs/user-guide/user-guides.html) for holding incremental
documentation guides, tutorials, and other content. `great-docs` automatically
detects, renders, and indexes any Quarto documents in this directory, so it's perfect
for our purposes. The `dev` directory is for holding prototype development notebooks
before they are ready to be added to the user guide. Think of it as a sandbox for your 
development work.
:::

Now you can go ahead and develop some Python code in the `dev` directory.
Let's create a module called `myfunc` that we want to tinker with, test and document.
To enable notebook-driven development, we want your Python code cells
to be exported to .py files that `great-docs` can then parse and document.
Create a new Quarto document in the `dev` directory called `myfunc.qmd`, 
and configure the extensions in your YAML frontmatter speficying where you 
want the `.py` files to be exported:

```yaml
# top of myfunc.qmd
---
title: "Module 1: `myfunc`"
subtitle: "An example function to demonstrate great-docs"
filters:
    - sorting-hat                            # selectively include/exclude cells on render
    - ripper                                 # extract code cells to script files
extensions:
    sorting-hat:
        keep:
            - python
    ripper:
        include-yaml: true                   # Include YAML as comments (default: true)
        output-name: "myfunc"                # Custom base name for py module file
        output-dir: "../src/great_docs_demo" # Directory to save scripts (relative to the .qmd file)
format: gfm                                  # render this dev notebook as markdown 
                                             # into the user guide, while simultaneously 
                                             # exporting code cells to .py files for 
                                             # great-docs to parse
---
```

And add some Python code you want to export to the notebook, with the appropriate docstrings
and some markdown to explain the code and its purpose.

```markdown
# plaintext in myfunc.qmd
I'm creating a function below... blah blah blah, etc.
```

We invoke `sorting-hat` using Quarto directives:

```python
# a code cell in myfunc.qmd
#| sorting-hat: keep
def myfunc(arg1, arg2, **kwargs):
    """
    Example function.

    Parameters
    ----------
    arg1 : int
    arg2 : str

    Returns
    -------
    None
    """
    return arg1 + arg2
```

The `sorting-hat: keep` directive communicates that this particular cell should be kept at export time.
To deliberately exclude a cell from export, use `sorting-hat: remove`. By default, 
all cells will be included in the export, so you only need to use `keep` or `discard` if you want to override 
that behavior for specific cells. `ripper` will then handle the export of the code cells to .py files.

::: {.callout-important}
Funny thing, you have to specify `sorting-hat` _before_ `ripper` because the order of extensions matters. If you 
specify `ripper` first, it will export all cells to .py files, defeating the purpose.
:::

To extract the code to .py files, first configure your root `_quarto.yml` file:

```yaml
# root _quarto.yml
project:
  title: "great-docs-demo"
  output-dir: "user_guide"    # the output directory for intermediate junk; gitignore this!
  render: 
    - "dev/*"                 # render the development notebooks for documentation
    - "!great-docs/**"        # DO NOT render great-docs directory to avoid circular rendering
    - "!*.md"
```

...and then run the following command:

```bash
quarto render
```
 
Now, take a look at your project:

```
(great-docs-demo) (base) ➜  great-docs-demo git:(main) ✗ tree -L 3 .      
.
├── README.md                 <- created by uv
├── _extensions               <- quarto extensions     
│   └── coatless-quarto
│       ├── ripper
│       └── sorting-hat
├── _quarto.yml               <- quarto configuration file
├── dev                       <- development notebooks
│   └── myfunc.qmd
├── great-docs-demo.qmd       <- quarto project landing page created by `quarto create`
├── great-docs.yml            <- great-docs configuration file created by `great-docs init`
├── pyproject.toml            <- created by uv
├── src                       <- source code for the Python package created by `uv init`
│   └── great_docs_demo
│       ├── __init__.py
│       ├── __pycache__
│       ├── myfunc.py         <- created by `ripper` extension
│       └── py.typed
├── user_guide                <- rendered documentation notebooks
│   └── dev
│       └── myfunc.md         <- rendered from myfunc.qmd by Quarto, with code cells exported to myfunc.py by `ripper`
└── uv.lock

11 directories, 11 files
```

At this point, you can now finally invoke `great-docs` to generate 
your documentation site. To do that, run:

```bash
great-docs build
```

If successful, you should see a message like this:

```
✅ Site built successfully

🔍 Generating SEO files...
   ⚠️  No base URL configured, skipping sitemap generation
      Set seo.canonical.base_url in great-docs.yml or configure a GitHub repo
   Generated robots.txt

🎉 Your site is ready! Open: /Users/tit420/projects/great-docs-demo/great-docs/_site/index.html
```

Now you can finally preview your documentation site with the API reference page:

```bash
great docs preview
```

And just like that, you have a documentation site for your Python package, with an API reference that was automatically generated from your docstrings!

![Great Docs Preview](great-docs-preview.png){group="my-gallery"}

The best part? `great-docs` adds the original notebooks from the `user_guide` directory 
to the documentation site as well, so users can see the code in its original context, 
and you can use the notebooks as a sort of "living documentation" that evolves with your code.

![Great Docs Reference Page](great-docs-reference.png){group="my-gallery"}

![Great Docs Function](great-docs-core-myfunc.png){group="my-gallery"}

Of course, don't forget to enable GitHub Pages for your repository to share your 
documentation with the world. Happy coding, and remember, documentation is not a chore, 
it's a love letter to your future self and your users. 
With a combination of notebook-driven development in `nbdev`, and the ease of use of 
`great-docs`, you can make that love letter as great as the code it describes. 💌✨
